question_text: The fourth general election of 1967 is widely considered the first real challenge to Congress dominance since independence. Which of the following best explains the multiple structural reasons behind Congress's reduced performance in this election?

options.text: Congress lost in 1967 solely because Nehru had died in 1964; his personal charisma was the only basis for Congress dominance and without him the party had nothing to offer voters

Congress's 1967 setback reflected a convergence of structural factors: two years of severe drought (1965-66) that caused food shortages and price rise, the humiliating 1966 rupee devaluation under World Bank pressure, the aftermath of the 1962 China defeat and 1965 Pakistan war eroding Congress's governance image, rising backward caste mobilisation that found Congress unresponsive, and anti-incumbency after 20 years of Congress rule
Congress lost because the Election Commission changed the voting rules to disadvantage Congress by requiring a majority (50%+) rather than a plurality to win seats
Congress lost because all opposition parties formed a single pre-election national coalition that fielded only one candidate per constituency, concentrating the anti-Congress vote

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - Congress actually won a Lok Sabha majority in 1967 under Indira Gandhi (Nehru's successor); it was a reduced majority not a defeat; and Congress's organisational strength, not only Nehru's charisma, had been the basis for its earlier dominance
Option C is incorrect - India has always used the first-past-the-post plurality system; no majority requirement was introduced; the electoral rules were unchanged

Option D is incorrect - there was no single pre-election national opposition coalition in 1967; the SVD coalitions were formed post-election; pre-election, opposition parties largely ran separately and their vote was fragmented
explanation: The 1967 elections represented the cumulative impact of several crises. Economic: India had suffered two consecutive monsoon failures (1965-66) causing severe food shortages; the government had to import grain under PL-480 humiliatingly; and the June 1966 rupee devaluation (57%, recommended by the World Bank and US as a condition for aid) was deeply unpopular. Security: the 1962 China defeat had permanently damaged India's military credibility; the 1965 Pakistan war ended in Tashkent concessions that the public viewed as surrendering military gains. Social: backward castes in UP, Bihar, and other states were increasingly organising through new vehicles (Ram Manohar Lohia's SSP, Charan Singh's BKD) that offered them platforms Congress did not. Political: after 20 years of Congress rule, anti-incumbency was building in many states. These factors together produced Congress's worst performance since 1952.
subject: political-science

question_text: The 1967 elections revealed a fundamental tension within Congress between its national leadership's positions and the social interests of dominant castes that controlled state-level Congress organisations. Which of the following most accurately describes this internal contradiction and its electoral consequences?

options.text: There was perfect harmony between Congress's national leadership and state Congress units in 1967; all Congress factions supported identical policies and presented a united front

Congress's national leadership (Nehru, later Indira Gandhi) advocated for progressive policies including land reform, backward class uplift, and secularism; but state Congress units were controlled by dominant landed castes (Jats in UP/Haryana, Reddys/Kammas in Andhra, Patidars in Gujarat) who blocked implementation of progressive policies; this created a credibility gap - Congress promised reform nationally but blocked it locally; backward caste voters who had trusted Congress's promises increasingly shifted to parties (Lohia's SSP, BKD) that put their interests first

State Congress units were more progressive than the national leadership; state-level Congress actually implemented aggressive land redistribution but the national Congress leadership was too conservative to endorse it
The tension was purely ideological - state Congress units were communist sympathisers who wanted nationalisation of all land, while the national leadership wanted free-market policies

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the Kamaraj Plan of 1963 was itself partly an attempt to address Congress's internal drift and loss of grassroots connect; calling it perfect harmony contradicts the well-documented factional tensions

Option C is incorrect - the documented failure of land ceiling legislation was precisely that state Congress units controlled by landlords had blocked it; the national leadership (under Nehru's Directive Principles) wanted reform but state units resisted; the option reverses the actual positions

Option D is incorrect - the tension was social and political (landed vs non-landed caste interests), not ideological in a communist-vs-free-market sense; state Congress leaders were not communist sympathisers
explanation: Congress's structure created a fundamental implementation problem. National Congress could promise land reform, OBC advancement, and progressive policies, but state PCCs (Pradesh Congress Committees) were dominated by exactly the social groups (dominant landed castes) that stood to lose from such reforms. Zamindari abolition was successfully implemented (the intermediary class had little political power), but land ceiling legislation failed because dominant farmers controlled state Congress units. As backward caste communities became more politically conscious in the 1960s through Lohia's political mobilisation, they realised that Congress's local power structure was working against their interests even when national Congress rhetoric was sympathetic. The 1967 elections registered this disillusionment, particularly in UP and Bihar where backward castes defected to the SSP and BKD.
subject: political-science

question_text: The anti-Hindi agitation of 1965 in Tamil Nadu was a significant event that influenced the 1967 elections in that state. Which of the following correctly explains the agitation's background and how it contributed to the DMK's landmark victory?

options.text: The 1965 anti-Hindi agitation was a protest against the use of English in Tamil Nadu; Tamil people demanded Hindi be replaced by Tamil as the sole medium of education

The Official Languages Act 1963 had stipulated that Hindi would become the sole official language of India from January 26, 1965; when this deadline approached, Tamil Nadu erupted in violent protests because Tamil speakers feared Hindi imposition would disadvantage them in competitive examinations and employment; the Congress government's handling of the agitation (police firing killed protesters) was deeply unpopular; the DMK which had been at the forefront of the protests swept Tamil Nadu in 1967
The 1965 anti-Hindi agitation was peaceful and quickly resolved by the Congress government through an immediate amendment making Tamil the sole official language of all India
The anti-Hindi agitation had no connection to the 1967 elections; the DMK won in Tamil Nadu purely because of corruption charges against the Congress government and had no linguistic dimension

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the agitation was against Hindi imposition, not against English; Tamil people wanted to retain English as an alternative to Hindi for pan-Indian communication, not replace English with Tamil

Option C is incorrect - the agitation was far from peaceful; police firing killed protesters including two students who self-immolated; the resolution was not making Tamil an all-India language but continuing English alongside Hindi

Option D is incorrect - the linguistic dimension was central to the DMK's 1967 victory; the DMK had articulated Tamil cultural identity, Dravidian pride, and resistance to Hindi imposition consistently since its founding
explanation: The anti-Hindi agitation of January-February 1965 was triggered by the approaching implementation of the Official Languages Act (1963), which provided for Hindi becoming the sole official language without the safeguard of continuing English that the States Reorganisation era had promised. Tamil speakers feared a double disadvantage: having to learn a third language (Hindi in addition to Tamil and English) while also competing against Hindi-speaking states in Union Public Service Commission examinations conducted in Hindi. The agitation became violent; police fired on protesters; two students (Veerappan and Aranganathan) self-immolated in protest. The Congress government's handling was viewed as insensitive to Tamil concerns. The Indira Gandhi government ultimately gave an assurance that English would continue as an official language for as long as non-Hindi states wanted. The DMK channelled this Tamil cultural grievance, sweeping the 1967 elections.
subject: political-science

question_text: The 1967 elections produced a significant fragmentation of the Lok Sabha. Which of the following correctly identifies the key numerical outcomes and their political significance?

options.text: Congress won 400 seats in 1967, its highest ever majority, demonstrating that criticism of Congress's governance failures was electorally irrelevant

Congress won 283 Lok Sabha seats out of 520 (54.4% of seats) with approximately 40.8% of the popular vote - a significant drop from 361 seats in 1962; simultaneously Congress lost power in nine states; while retaining the national government, the results showed the Congress System's equilibrium had fractured
Congress lost the 1967 Lok Sabha election to the opposition Janata Dal, which formed the national government; 1967 was the first non-Congress national government in Indian history
The 1967 election had only two significant parties - Congress and the CPI; all other parties won fewer than 5 seats combined

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - Congress won 283 seats, not 400; it was the party's worst national performance to date, not its best

Option C is incorrect - Congress retained the national government under Indira Gandhi after 1967; the Janata Party (which formed the first non-Congress national government) only came to power in 1977 after the Emergency

Option D is incorrect - multiple parties won significant numbers of seats: Jana Sangh (35), Swatantra (18), CPI+CPI(M) (around 42 combined), SSP, BKD, DMK etc.; the election produced a more genuinely competitive parliament than ever before
explanation: The February-March 1967 fourth general election produced a parliament that was visibly more plural than the 1952-62 Nehru era. Congress: 283/520 seats (54.4%), 40.8% vote share - down from 361/494 (73.1%) in 1962. Jana Sangh: 35 seats. Swatantra: 18. CPI: 23. CPI(M): 19. SSP: 23. The combined non-Congress opposition exceeded 237 seats. More dramatically, nine state assemblies simultaneously returned non-Congress governments: Tamil Nadu (DMK), Kerala (CPI(M)-led Left Front), West Bengal (United Front), Punjab (Akali Dal-Congress breakaway), Rajasthan, UP, Bihar, Madhya Pradesh, and Haryana (various SVD coalitions). This dual outcome - retained national majority but state losses - demonstrated that Congress dominance was neither total nor permanent.
subject: political-science

question_text: The 1967 elections triggered what political scientists call a 'political earthquake' that permanently changed Indian electoral politics. Which of the following most accurately evaluates the elections' long-term structural significance?

options.text: The 1967 elections had no long-term significance because Congress quickly recovered its dominant position in 1971; the elections were merely a temporary blip in an otherwise continuous Congress dominance

The 1967 elections were structurally significant because they demonstrated: first, that regional parties could permanently displace Congress in states with strong sub-national identities (DMK in Tamil Nadu); second, that backward caste assertion could fracture Congress's multi-caste coalition; third, that opposition parties could overcome ideological differences to form state-level coalitions; and fourth, that elections could produce genuine policy accountability through competitive politics even in a previously one-party-dominant system

The 1967 elections demonstrated that India needed to return to a one-party system; the instability produced by competitive elections convinced all major parties to negotiate a formal power-sharing arrangement to prevent future electoral uncertainty
The 1967 elections were significant only in showing that foreign powers (particularly the US and CIA) could manipulate Indian elections; the anti-Congress vote was entirely manufactured externally

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - while Congress did recover in 1971, the 1967 elections permanently changed the terrain; Tamil Nadu has never returned to Congress dominance since 1967; backward caste politics permanently transformed UP and Bihar; these are structural changes, not temporary blips

Option C is incorrect - no power-sharing arrangement was negotiated; competitive elections continued; and the instability of SVD coalitions itself reflected the vitality of competitive democracy, not its failure

Option D is incorrect - there is no credible historical evidence of US/CIA manipulation producing the 1967 anti-Congress vote; the economic grievances (drought, devaluation) and social mobilisation (backward caste assertion) were genuine domestic factors
explanation: The 1967 elections' structural legacy is significant. The DMK's 1967 Tamil Nadu victory was permanent - Dravidian parties (DMK and later AIADMK) have governed Tamil Nadu continuously since then, with Congress never returning to power there. This established that in states with strong regional/linguistic identities, regional parties could permanently replace national parties. In UP and Bihar, backward caste mobilisation (through SSP and BKD) began a process that culminated in the Mandal-era politics of the 1990s and SP/BSP dominance. The SVD coalition experience, while unstable, taught politicians and voters that coalition politics was possible - experience that proved essential for the multi-party coalition era of 1989-2014. The 1967 elections also showed that Congress's organisational strength could not indefinitely override economic grievances and social dissatisfaction.
subject: political-science

question_text: C.N. Annadurai (Anna), the DMK leader who became Tamil Nadu's first non-Congress Chief Minister after the 1967 elections, had a distinctive political ideology. Which of the following correctly describes the DMK's ideological platform that resonated so powerfully with Tamil voters?

options.text: The DMK's platform was purely economic - it promised Tamil Nadu's industrialisation and had no cultural or linguistic dimension; its victory was entirely about economic grievances

The DMK combined Dravidian cultural nationalism (celebrating Tamil language, literature, and non-Brahmin Dravidian identity), social reformist tradition (inherited from Periyar's Self-Respect Movement opposing Brahmin ritual dominance), opposition to Hindi imposition, advocacy for state autonomy within a federal India, and populist welfare promises; this integrated cultural-social-political platform addressed multiple aspects of Tamil identity simultaneously
The DMK was a communist party that sought to establish a socialist republic of Tamil Nadu independent from India; its 1967 victory was a step toward secession
The DMK's platform was identical to Congress's; it differed only in being led by Tamil film personalities rather than traditional politicians; there was no substantive ideological distinction

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the DMK's cultural and linguistic dimension was central, not peripheral; the anti-Hindi agitation (1965) and Dravidian cultural assertion were explicitly the DMK's core mobilising issues alongside economic concerns

Option C is incorrect - the DMK was not a communist or secessionist party; it had abandoned its earlier demand for Dravida Nadu (separate Dravidian state) in 1963 and committed to Indian federalism; its platform was sub-national cultural autonomy within India, not secession

Option D is incorrect - the DMK had a highly developed and distinct ideology rooted in Periyar's rationalism, Dravidian identity, and anti-Brahmin social reform; this was substantively different from Congress's multi-caste nationalist ideology
explanation: The DMK (Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, founded 1949 by C.N. Annadurai after splitting from Periyar's DK) integrated multiple strands of Tamil political thought. The Dravidian movement traced from Periyar's Self-Respect Movement (1925) which challenged Brahmin ritual dominance and Sanskrit's cultural hegemony. The DMK added to this: resistance to Hindi imposition as a form of cultural colonialism by north Indian states; advocacy for genuine federalism that would give Tamil Nadu substantial autonomy; and a distinctly Tamil cultural renaissance through cinema (C.N. Annadurai and M. Karunanidhi were both scriptwriters and filmmakers who used popular culture as a political medium). Anna's personal qualities - his eloquent Tamil oratory, moral simplicity, and genuine accessibility - gave the party a charismatic face. His death in office in February 1969 made him Tamil Nadu's most revered politician.
subject: political-science

question_text: The 1967 elections saw the emergence of coalitional politics at the state level. Which of the following correctly describes the nature of the Samyukta Vidhayak Dal (SVD) coalitions that came to power in several states?

options.text: SVD coalitions were pre-election alliances where parties agreed on common minimum programmes and policy priorities before campaigning jointly against Congress

SVD coalitions were post-election assemblages of ideologically diverse parties - combining Jana Sangh (Hindu nationalist), CPI (communist), SSP (socialist), BKD (agrarian), Swatantra (free market), and independents - formed solely to prevent Congress from forming a government; they had no ideological coherence or agreed programme
SVD coalitions were Congress-led coalitions that brought in smaller regional parties to support a Congress minority government in states where Congress had won the largest share of seats
SVD coalitions were formally registered as a single political party before the 1967 elections and contested as a united front with a common symbol

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - SVDs were explicitly post-election formations; no pre-election common programmes were agreed; the ideological incompatibility (Jana Sangh and CPI in the same coalition) made pre-election agreement essentially impossible
Option C is incorrect - SVDs were anti-Congress formations, not Congress-led; their entire raison d'etre was to prevent Congress from holding power in states Congress had not won a majority
Option D is incorrect - SVDs were not registered as parties; they were informal parliamentary coalitions of separately-contesting parties that came together only after election results were known
explanation: The Samyukta Vidhayak Dal (United Legislature Party) coalitions of 1967 were remarkable exercises in what political scientists call 'negative coalitions' - formed around a shared opposition to a single party rather than around positive programmatic agreement. UP had a coalition government under Charan Singh that included the SSP, BKD, Jana Sangh, CPI, Independents, and other groups. Bihar had a similar heterogeneous alliance. These coalitions governed briefly but were inherently unstable: without common policy goals, they depended on personal deals among leaders; defections constantly threatened survival; within months most had collapsed. Some formed and reformed two or three times. The 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' (come and go) phenomenon of MLAs switching parties multiple times - exemplified by Haryana's Gaya Lal who switched parties three times in fifteen days - characterised this era.
subject: political-science

question_text: Political scientist Rajni Kothari described the 1967 elections as the 'end of the Congress System' but subsequent events showed this was premature. Which of the following most accurately evaluates whether 1967 represented an end or a transformation of Congress dominance?

options.text: Kothari was completely correct; 1967 ended Congress dominance permanently; Congress has not won a national majority since 1967

The 1967 elections marked a transformation rather than an end: the old Congress System (organisational dominance through patronage networks and multi-caste coalitions) was irreparably damaged, but Congress reinvented its dominance under Indira Gandhi through a new model of personalised populist mobilisation ('Garibi Hatao') that produced an even larger majority in 1971 - suggesting 1967 was a transition between two modes of Congress dominance rather than the end of Congress itself
The 1967 elections had no effect on Congress's position; the party's dominance was completely unchanged and the Rajni Kothari description was entirely wrong at the time
Congress's 1967 reversal was temporary; by the 1968 by-elections Congress had already fully recovered its pre-1967 position in all states where it had lost

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - Congress won 352 seats in 1971 and 415 seats in 1984, both larger than any Nehru-era majority; Indira Gandhi won national majorities, disproving permanent end

Option C is incorrect - the 1967 results were a significant change from the three previous elections; nine state losses and reduced national majority were genuine changes even if temporary nationally; calling Kothari's observation 'completely wrong' overstates
Option D is incorrect - Congress did not immediately recover in all states where it had lost; Tamil Nadu returned to DMK permanently; UP Bihar Congress dominance was permanently fractured
explanation: The 1967-1971 period shows a transformation, not a simple end. Kothari's 1967 'end of Congress System' paper was prescient about the end of the OLD system (balanced umbrella party with internal ideological diversity managed by Syndicate bosses). But Congress's 1971 victory under Indira Gandhi showed that Indian electoral dominance could be rebuilt on new foundations: direct mass appeal bypassing party intermediaries, populist economic messaging ('Garibi Hatao' vs 'Indira Hatao'), and emotional identification between a charismatic leader and the poor. This new model of Congress dominance was actually more electorally potent than the old (352 seats in 1971 vs ~361 in 1962) but also more brittle - it depended entirely on Indira's personal popularity rather than organisational depth, which is why its reversal in 1977 (post-Emergency) was equally dramatic.
subject: political-science

question_text: The 1967 elections saw significant gains by the Jana Sangh (35 seats) and Swatantra Party (18 seats). What do these gains reveal about the political landscape that was emerging in Indian democracy?

options.text: The Jana Sangh and Swatantra gains were statistical accidents with no political meaning; both parties declined to zero seats in subsequent elections

The gains of both parties revealed that ideological alternatives to Congress's left-of-centre socialism were finding electoral traction: Jana Sangh represented Hindu cultural nationalism that was marginalised but not extinct in Indian politics, while Swatantra represented free-market liberalism; together their gains signalled that India's political spectrum was wider than Congress's dominant left-nationalist consensus had suggested
The Jana Sangh and Swatantra gains were identical in nature; both parties had identical ideologies and voter bases and were essentially the same political force
The Jana Sangh's gains demonstrated that all Hindu voters had abandoned Congress permanently; no Hindu voter supported Congress after 1967

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - Jana Sangh continued to grow, winning 35 seats in 1967 and eventually merging into the Janata Party that won the 1977 elections; its successor BJP became India's dominant party by 2014

Option C is incorrect - Jana Sangh and Swatantra were ideologically distinct: Jana Sangh was Hindu cultural nationalist, socially conservative, and supported a Hindu India concept; Swatantra was secular free-market liberal; their voter bases were different (urban lower-middle class for Jana Sangh, landlords and merchants for Swatantra)
Option D is incorrect - Congress continued to receive substantial Hindu votes; Hindu voters are not a monolithic bloc; millions of Hindus voted for Congress in 1971 giving it 352 seats
explanation: The 1967 gains by Jana Sangh and Swatantra signalled the emergence of credible right-of-centre politics in India. Jana Sangh (35 seats) performed particularly well in urban Hindi-belt constituencies - among traders, artisans, and lower-middle-class Hindus who felt their cultural identity was not represented by Congress's secular-leftist coalition. Swatantra's 18 seats came from a coalition of Rajput landlords, former princely state supporters, and business interests that opposed the economic statism of Nehru's planning model. While both parties would eventually merge into the Janata coalition (1977), their 1967 gains showed that right-of-centre Hindu nationalism and economic liberalism were constituencies that could be mobilised. The long-term trajectory led from Jana Sangh's 1967 performance through the BJP's founding in 1980 to its dominant position today - making 1967's Jana Sangh results a precursor to a much larger transformation.
subject: political-science

question_text: The period 1967-71 in Indian state politics was characterised by extreme political instability. Which of the following best explains how this instability affected both democratic norms and legislative effectiveness?

options.text: The instability of 1967-71 was entirely beneficial for democracy; frequent government changes kept all parties accountable and produced better policy outcomes than stable Congress governments had

The instability created a contradictory legacy: it demonstrated India's democratic resilience by showing power could change hands, but defection politics (Aaya Ram Gaya Ram) severely damaged legislative integrity and governance; SVD governments were too preoccupied with political survival to implement meaningful policy; President's Rule became overused as a crisis management tool; and the experience ultimately generated demand for the anti-defection law (1985) as a constitutional corrective
Political instability in 1967-71 had no effect on legislation or governance; all SVD governments implemented their full policy programmes and legislated effectively despite defection threats
The instability caused permanent damage to Indian democracy from which it never recovered; after 1967 India was unable to hold free and fair elections

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - frequent government changes do not automatically produce better policy; when governments change every few months due to floor-crossing, they lack both the time and the political will to implement long-term policy programmes

Option C is incorrect - SVD governments were typically consumed by political survival arithmetic; the Aaya Ram Gaya Ram phenomenon showed that MLAs were focused on personal advancement rather than legislation; calling it full policy implementation contradicts the documented instability
Option D is incorrect - India successfully conducted free and fair elections in 1971, 1972, 1977 and beyond; the instability was a quality-of-governance problem, not a terminal democratic failure
explanation: The 1967-71 instability had a complex democratic legacy. Democratic gains: opposition parties governing multiple states proved power could change hands without violence or constitutional breakdown. Legislative costs: SVD governments in UP spent months dealing with confidence votes; Uttar Pradesh under Charan Singh fell within months, leading to President's Rule; Bihar had multiple governments; effective legislation was minimal. The defection crisis: Haryana's Gaya Lal exemplified how MLAs could switch parties repeatedly, reducing political accountability to near-zero. Article 356 (President's Rule) was invoked 9 times in 1967-71 to manage collapsed SVD governments. The positive outcome: this chaotic experience created the political will for constitutional reform, culminating in the 52nd Amendment (1985) inserting the Tenth Schedule (anti-defection law) under Rajiv Gandhi.
concept_tags: Aaya Ram Gaya Ram institutional response
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following key figures or parties from the 1967 elections and their aftermath with their correct description of role or significance.

column_b.text: Congress PM who retained national power after the 1967 election with a reduced majority; subsequently challenged the Syndicate through bank nationalisation and the 1969 presidential election to establish personal dominance over the party
Founder of the DMK; first non-Congress Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu after the historic 1967 state election; died in office February 1969; succeeded by M. Karunanidhi
UP Congress breakaway leader; formed Bharatiya Kranti Dal (BKD); became UP CM through SVD coalition; championed Jat peasant and backward caste interests; later briefly PM in 1979
Haryana independent MLA who changed his party affiliation three times within fifteen days in 1967, giving birth to the phrase 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' that symbolised defection politics

options.text: i-Q, ii-R, iii-S, iv-P
i-S, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-P
i-Q, ii-P, iii-S, iv-R
i-R, ii-Q, iii-S, iv-P

elimination_hints: C.N. Annadurai's unique identifiers are 'DMK founder', 'first non-Congress Tamil Nadu CM 1967', 'died February 1969', and 'succeeded by Karunanidhi' - only option Q contains all these; the February 1969 death and Karunanidhi succession are distinctive details
Gaya Lal's unique identifier is 'three times fifteen days 1967 Haryana' and 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram phrase origin' - option S is the only one that specifically describes this famous incident
Charan Singh's unique identifiers are 'BKD founder', 'UP CM through SVD coalition', 'Jat peasant backward caste', and 'briefly PM 1979' - option R uniquely combines BKD with the 1979 brief PM role
explanation: C.N. Annadurai (1909-1969): 'Anna' - Tamil for elder brother/respected elder; DMK founder and Tamil Nadu's transformative CM; his death triggered a massive outpouring of grief and elevated him to quasi-deity status in Tamil politics. Charan Singh (1902-1987): founded BKD in 1967; his SVD-led government in UP was India's first significant backward caste-led government; became PM briefly in July 1979 but never faced Parliament before resigning. Gaya Lal: Haryana independent MLA; his three party-switches in fifteen days gave the term 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' its specific political meaning. Indira Gandhi: retained national PM position post-1967; her subsequent actions (bank nationalisation, presidential election manoeuvre) showed she intended to dominate Congress rather than be dominated by the Syndicate.
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following states with the correct description of the political outcome of the 1967 elections and what party or coalition came to power.

column_b.text: SVD coalition under Charan Singh; first non-Congress government in this state; BKD, SSP, Jana Sangh, CPI and others united to exclude Congress; fell quickly due to defections; Congress returned under President's Rule
DMK under C.N. Annadurai won 138 of 234 assembly seats; first non-Congress government in south India; permanent shift away from Congress - no Congress government has returned since

United Front coalition government including CPI(M) and other left parties; CPM's Jyoti Basu was a key figure; politically volatile with Congress trying to destabilise; significant naxalite movement emerged in this period
Akali Dal-led coalition government; the Punjabi Suba demand having just been fulfilled (1966) created conditions for Akali Dal to compete more effectively; first Akali-led government in the state

options.text: i-Q, ii-R, iii-S, iv-P
i-R, ii-Q, iii-S, iv-P
i-Q, ii-P, iii-S, iv-R
i-S, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-P

elimination_hints: Tamil Nadu's unique feature is 'DMK 138/234 seats', 'permanent shift', and 'no Congress government since' - only option Q describes both the DMK landslide and the permanence of the shift; no other option mentions 138 seats
West Bengal's unique feature is 'United Front', 'CPI(M) Jyoti Basu', and 'naxalite movement emerged' - option R uniquely combines United Front with CPI(M) and the naxalite context

Punjab's unique feature is 'Akali Dal', '1966 Punjabi Suba just fulfilled', and 'first Akali-led government' - option S uniquely identifies the Akali Dal win in context of the just-fulfilled Punjabi Suba demand
explanation: Tamil Nadu: DMK's overwhelming victory (138/234 seats) ended Congress dominance permanently; C.N. Annadurai became CM; Tamil Nadu has been governed exclusively by Dravidian parties (DMK and AIADMK) since. West Bengal: United Front government, initially without CPI(M) in the CM chair (but they were a key pillar); Jyoti Basu was a deputy CM figure; 1967-68 saw the Naxalbari uprising (spring thunder) that launched the naxalite movement. Punjab: The Punjab Reorganisation Act (1966) had just created Haryana and a Punjabi-speaking Punjab; Akali Dal capitalised on this, forming a coalition government under Gurnam Singh - the first Akali Dal-led Punjab government. Uttar Pradesh: Charan Singh's BKD-led SVD coalition was India's first significant backward caste-led government, but lasted only months before falling to defections.
concept_tags: UP Charan Singh SVD first backward caste
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following electoral or political concepts from the 1967 election period with their correct definition and significance in Indian political history.

column_b.text: The 1967-71 period showed a shift from the quasi-bipolar system (Congress vs fragmented opposition) of the 1950s-60s toward genuine multi-party competition where multiple parties could plausibly form state governments

The tendency of voters to vote against the incumbent party after extended periods of governance due to accumulated grievances; the 1967 election illustrated how 20 years of Congress rule had built up resentment particularly on economic issues

Constitutional provision allowing central government to dismiss state governments when constitutional governance breaks down; used excessively in 1967-72 to manage collapsed SVD coalitions; subsequently restricted by the Bommai judgment (1994)

The practice of elected legislators switching parties, sometimes multiple times; illustrated by the Aaya Ram Gaya Ram phenomenon; eventually addressed by the Tenth Schedule anti-defection law (52nd Amendment, 1985)

options.text: i-Q, ii-S, iii-R, iv-P
i-S, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-P
i-Q, ii-R, iii-S, iv-P
i-P, ii-S, iii-R, iv-Q

elimination_hints: Anti-incumbency's defining feature is 'vote against incumbent party after extended governance' and '20 years Congress resentment' - only option Q specifically identifies this definition with the '20 years' Congress example

Floor-crossing/defection's defining features are 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' and 'Tenth Schedule 52nd Amendment 1985' - option S uniquely combines both the specific phenomenon name and the constitutional remedy

President's Rule's defining features are 'Article 356', 'collapsed SVD coalitions', and 'Bommai judgment 1994' - option R uniquely identifies all three; the Bommai judgment restricting Article 356 is a distinctive detail
explanation: Anti-incumbency (the Q concept): by 1967, Congress had governed at the national and most state levels for 20 years; voters with economic grievances (drought, devaluation) held the incumbent Congress responsible, demonstrating the anti-incumbency dynamic that subsequently shaped Indian elections. Floor-crossing/defection (the S concept): Aaya Ram Gaya Ram era showed how the absence of anti-defection rules allowed legislators to be traded for ministerial positions; the 1985 Tenth Schedule was the constitutional corrective. Article 356 (the R concept): the 'President's Rule' provision became a tool to manage unstable SVD coalitions; the S.R. Bommai case (1994) required Supreme Court approval before dissolution, restricting its misuse. Multi-party competition (the P concept): 1967 marked the beginning of the end of quasi-bipolar politics and the emergence of genuine multi-party competition at the state level.
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): The 1967 fourth general election represented a complete rejection by Indian voters of the Congress party and its ideology, marking the permanent beginning of a post-Congress era in Indian politics.
Reason (R): In the 1967 election, Congress retained its national Lok Sabha majority with 283 seats (reduced from 361 in 1962) and continued to govern at the national level under PM Indira Gandhi; Congress's 1971 election saw an even larger majority of 352 seats under the 'Garibi Hatao' campaign.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false: 1967 did NOT represent a complete rejection marking a permanent post-Congress era. Congress retained its national majority and won a massive 352-seat national majority just four years later (1971), and again won 415 seats in 1984. The Reason is true: it accurately states that Congress kept its national majority in 1967 with 283 seats and then grew it to 352 in 1971. These facts directly refute the Assertion's claim of 'complete rejection' and 'permanent beginning of post-Congress era.' Since A is false and R correctly describes the evidence that proves A false, the answer is D.
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): The 1965-66 drought and subsequent food crisis was the single most important factor in Congress's reduced 1967 election performance.
Reason (R): Economic hardship directly reduces support for incumbent governments because voters hold the government responsible for food prices, employment, and living standards, and seek alternatives when these deteriorate.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false: the drought and food crisis was AN important factor but likely not the SINGLE most important. Multiple equally significant factors contributed: the 1966 rupee devaluation (57%) was politically devastating; the 1962 China defeat had permanently damaged Congress's image; backward caste mobilisation (SSP, BKD) was a structural social shift; 20 years of anti-incumbency; and regional cultural challenges (Tamil Nadu's anti-Hindi agitation). Calling any single factor 'the' most important is an overstatement that the multi-causal historical record refutes. The Reason is true: economic hardship does reduce incumbent support through voter attribution of responsibility. Since A is false (single-factor explanation is too narrow) and R is true (accurately describes the economic-voting relationship), the answer is D.
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): The DMK's victory in Tamil Nadu in 1967 demonstrated that regional parties based on cultural and linguistic identity are inherently secessionist and incompatible with Indian national unity.
Reason (R): The DMK had abandoned its earlier demand for a separate Dravida Nadu state in 1963, committed to working within the Indian constitutional framework, and governed Tamil Nadu as a state within the Indian Union after 1967 without attempting secession.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false: the DMK's victory did not demonstrate that cultural-linguistic regional parties are inherently secessionist. The Reason directly refutes it - the DMK had explicitly abandoned its Dravida Nadu demand in 1963 and governed Tamil Nadu as a legitimate state government within India. The DMK (and subsequent Dravidian governments) have been among India's most constitutionally compliant regional governments, using Tamil Nadu's position within the federation to advance Tamil interests without secession. The Assertion conflates sub-national cultural assertion with secessionism, which is a conceptual error - cultural federalism (demanding autonomy and cultural respect within a federation) is quite different from separatism. Since A is false and R correctly explains why (DMK explicitly renounced secessionism and governed constitutionally), the answer is D.
subject: political-science

question_text:
Examine the following statements about the 1967 elections and their immediate aftermath, then identify which combination is correct:
(i) Congress won 283 Lok Sabha seats in 1967, retaining a national majority but with a significantly reduced margin compared to its 1962 performance of 361 seats.
(ii) The DMK under C.N. Annadurai won the Tamil Nadu state assembly elections in 1967, becoming the first non-Congress state government in south India; this shift has proved permanent.
(iii) The Samyukta Vidhayak Dal (SVD) coalitions formed in several states after 1967 were pre-election alliances where parties agreed on a common minimum programme before campaigning together.
(iv) The anti-Hindi agitation of January-February 1965 in Tamil Nadu was a significant factor in mobilising Tamil sentiment against Congress and in favour of the DMK's 1967 victory.

options.text: Only (i), (ii), and (iv) are correct
Only (ii) and (iii) are correct
All four statements are correct
(i), (iii), and (iv) are correct; (ii) is incorrect

explanation: Statement (i) is TRUE: Congress won 283 seats in 1967 (vs 361 in 1962), retaining a national majority. Statement (ii) is TRUE: DMK's Tamil Nadu victory was the first non-Congress south Indian government, and has proved permanent - no Congress government has returned since. Statement (iii) is FALSE: SVDs were post-election coalitions formed after results were known, not pre-election alliances with common minimum programmes; the ideological incompatibility of their members (Jana Sangh and CPI in the same coalition) made pre-election agreement impossible. Statement (iv) is TRUE: the 1965 anti-Hindi agitation and Congress government's handling of protests (police firing) significantly mobilised Tamil sentiment toward DMK. Only (i), (ii), and (iv) are correct.
subject: political-science

question_text: The table below compares Congress's performance across four elections (1952, 1957, 1962, 1967) on five dimensions: national seat share, national vote share, states where Congress held power, evidence of electoral coalition stability, and the nature of opposition competition. Based on a careful reading of the trends and the 1967 inflection point, which conclusion is most strongly supported?

options.text: The data shows Congress strengthening in every dimension from 1952 through 1967, with 1967 being its strongest performance

The data reveals that the 1967 election represented a multi-dimensional simultaneous weakening across all five indicators: seat share fell, vote share fell, state governments were lost, the multi-caste coalition showed cracks, and opposition competition became more organised; the convergence of weakness across all dimensions simultaneously suggests a structural fracture rather than merely a bad election cycle - though the national majority's retention shows the fracture was incomplete
The data shows that only the national seat share changed in 1967; all other dimensions remained stable, suggesting the election was a minor statistical variation rather than a structural shift
The data shows that the 1967 election was entirely determined by the weather (drought); once weather improved, all dimensions returned to pre-1967 levels in subsequent elections

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the data clearly shows Congress declining from 361 to 283 seats between 1962 and 1967; nine state governments lost; vote share falling from 44.7% to 40.8%; calling it 'strengthening' directly contradicts the data

Option C is incorrect - multiple dimensions changed: seat share, vote share, state governments, opposition organisation all shifted; reducing it to only seat share changing ignores the state-level losses and coalition fractures documented in the table

Option D is incorrect - Tamil Nadu's permanent DMK shift, backward caste politics in UP and Bihar, and the defection phenomenon were structural social changes, not weather-reversible; calling it all weather-driven ignores the social mobilisation data
explanation: The four-election comparison reveals why 1967 was a structural inflection point. 1952-1962: Congress held 73-75% of seats with 44-48% of votes across all three elections; Congress held all major state governments; opposition was fragmented (no state lost to opposition); multi-caste coalition appeared stable. 1967: seats fell to 54.4% despite only modest vote share fall (40.8%); nine state governments lost simultaneously; opposition parties won in diverse geographic and ideological contexts (left in West Bengal, Dravidian in Tamil Nadu, agrarian in UP); and the coalition showed demographic cracks (backward caste defection to SSP/BKD). The multi-dimensionality is the key analytical point: when an indicator falls in isolation, it may be a one-time event; when five indicators move in the same direction simultaneously, it suggests a structural shift. The retention of a national majority (283/520) shows the shift was incomplete - the Congress System fractured but did not collapse in 1967.
subject: political-science
